note_data.title: International Trade

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Break down the cause and effect relationships, showing how one factor leads to another
Present arguments for and against, then reach a reasoned conclusion

modelAnswer.question: Explain two benefits of international trade for Pakistan. [4 marks]
modelAnswer.answer: One benefit is access to a wider variety of goods and services. Pakistan can import goods that it cannot produce efficiently, such as advanced technology and machinery, giving consumers more choice. Another benefit is increased economic efficiency through specialisation. Pakistan can focus on producing goods where it has comparative advantage, such as textiles and rice, and trade these for goods other countries produce more efficiently, leading to higher total output and economic growth.
markBreakdown: Mark 1: Identifies benefit - access to wider variety of goods
Mark 2: Explains how this benefits Pakistan - more consumer choice
Mark 3: Identifies second benefit - specialisation/efficiency gains
Mark 4: Explains how specialisation leads to higher output/growth

note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Supply and demand diagram showing effect of tariffs - domestic supply and demand curves with world price line, showing tariff raising price from world price to domestic price, creating deadweight loss triangle and government revenue rectangle
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P1 Q15 (1 mark) — they asked students to identify what might reduce international trade in cereals, focusing on barriers to trade
This topic appeared in O/N 2022 P2 Q4 (8 marks) — students had to assess whether arguments for protectionism are always stronger than arguments against, requiring balanced evaluation
This concept was examined in M/J 2024 P1 Q12 (1 mark) — candidates needed to determine what might decrease when a closed economy allows international trade
This was tested in O/N 2023 P2 Q3 (6 marks) — students analyzed the impact of removing trade barriers on domestic industries and consumer welfare

workedExample.question: Assess whether the arguments for protectionism in international trade are always stronger than the arguments against. (8 marks)
steps: Step 1: Define protectionism as government policies that restrict international trade to protect domestic industries from foreign competition
Step 2: Present arguments FOR protectionism - infant industry protection, employment protection, strategic industries, balance of payments improvement
Step 3: Present arguments AGAINST protectionism - consumer welfare loss, reduced competition, retaliation risks, inefficient resource allocation
Step 4: Evaluate using real examples and consider different economic situations where one set of arguments might be stronger
Step 5: Conclude with balanced judgment acknowledging that strength of arguments depends on economic circumstances
workedExample.answer: Arguments for protectionism include protecting infant industries from unfair competition, maintaining employment in declining sectors, and safeguarding strategic industries vital for national security. However, arguments against protectionism are often stronger as free trade increases consumer choice, reduces prices, promotes efficiency through competition, and maximizes global welfare. The strength of arguments depends on economic circumstances - protectionism may be justified temporarily for infant industries but generally, free trade arguments are stronger for long-term economic growth.
workedExample.markScheme: 2 marks for clear arguments for protectionism, 2 marks for clear arguments against, 2 marks for evaluation with examples, 2 marks for balanced conclusion

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused absolute and comparative advantage' — always explain that comparative advantage, not absolute advantage, determines trade patterns
Examiner Report 2022: 'Students failed to distinguish between different types of trade barriers' — clearly differentiate between tariffs, quotas, subsidies and non-tariff barriers in your answers
Examiner Report 2024: 'Weak evaluation in protectionism questions' — always consider both short-term and long-term effects, and use real-world examples to support your analysis
note_data.keyDiagram: Supply and Demand diagram showing impact of tariffs: Draw domestic supply and demand curves intersecting at equilibrium. Add world price line below domestic equilibrium. Show how tariff raises world price, creating areas representing: consumer surplus loss (trapezium above world price), producer surplus gain (rectangle below domestic supply), government revenue (rectangle), and deadweight loss (two triangles). Label all areas clearly and explain welfare effects.
